source_file: 2022-10-09_Richard Lawson_The Eighteenth Sunday after Pentecost at the Wilderness.mp3
full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Welcome to this first Sunday at the Wilderness and a new series called Trinity in Motion,<|7.24|> <|7.24|> which will take place over the course of the next three weeks.<|11.24|> <|11.86|> During this season, we'll be praying with Andrei Rublev's icon, the Trinity, which is<|16.94|> <|16.94|> found, of course, on the front page of your leaflet.<|20.20|> <|20.20|> Thank you.<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as we do so, we'll seek to embody these great mysteries of the Nicene Creed,<|5.76|> <|5.94|> which is one of the great summarizations of the Christian faith.<|9.26|> <|10.16|> I want to thank Andrew or Aaron Nyquist and the people of Good Shepherd in New York for inspiring this series.<|16.90|> <|17.72|> I'm seated because in lieu of a conventional sermon,<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The clergy and I will share just a short teaching seated,<|4.18|> <|4.76|> and perhaps if we're seated, it will be short,<|7.50|> <|7.74|> followed by the open space for prayers,<|11.24|> <|11.28|> at which time you're invited to wander the cathedral,<|13.76|> <|14.82|> being conscious, especially during the season,<|16.92|> <|16.92|> that you are guided and held by God, the Holy Trinity.<|21.94|> <|22.72|> We begin our reflection with the first line in the creed,<|26.22|> <|26.30|> we believe in God, the Father Almighty.<|28.36|> <|29.08|> Amen.<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we sink into God, the Holy Trinity, it's important to remember that belief is never mere<|8.40|> <|8.40|> intellectual assent. Instead, belief is a choice to trust, always a choice.<|15.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Rowan Williams, the Welch poet and former Archbishop of Canterbury has said that the<|5.36|> <|5.36|> Creed is a series of statements about where I find the anchorage of my life, where I find<|12.96|> <|12.96|> solid ground, where I find home.<|16.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So when we say then we believe in one God, what we're saying really is that we find our home in God, that we're anchored in God, who is our refuge, our maker, our creator, the one who holds us always.<|18.38|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And for those of us in the Anglican and Episcopal tradition, this confidence, this trust, refuge,<|7.26|> <|7.38|> and home find their expression through us, but they have their genesis in God.<|14.34|> <|15.06|> Before we ourselves trust God, we're going to be a part of the world.<|18.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God trusts us. Before we are ever confident in God, God is actually confident in us.<|8.72|> <|9.30|> Before we take refuge in God, God has already taken refuge in us.<|14.92|> <|14.92|> Before we return home to God, God is already making God's home with us and in us.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And now to the language of Father.<|3.08|> <|4.08|> When we refer to God as the Father, the Almighty,<|6.58|> <|7.72|> are we saying that God is literally a man above us in heaven?<|13.10|> <|14.76|> No, all of our language for and about God is,<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is an approximation, always an approximation, even when it's found in the Creed. This language gets<|8.34|> <|8.34|> us to the edge of where God dwells. No string of words, no piece of music, no series of statements<|16.80|> <|16.80|> adequately articulates the ineffability of the divine.<|22.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So instead of literalizing this imagery, the 20th century writer Marion Mix says the following,<|7.38|> <|7.72|> for our purposes, the important thing to notice is the invitation that the title God the Father<|15.02|> <|15.02|> offers us. And the invitation is this, come closer.<|19.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These two words come closer may very well be the invitation from God for all of us this evening,<|7.34|> <|7.74|> the good God who's overflowing with goodness towards us and the whole cosmos,<|13.04|> <|13.42|> the God who uses almighty power to fill the entire creation and even our lives with goodness.<|22.22|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thanks.<|0.50|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Welcome to this first Sunday at the Wilderness and a new series called Trinity in Motion,<|7.24|>
<|7.24|> which will take place over the course of the next three weeks.<|11.24|>
<|11.86|> During this season, we'll be praying with Andrei Rublev's icon, the Trinity, which is<|16.94|>
<|16.94|> found, of course, on the front page of your leaflet.<|20.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as we do so, we'll seek to embody these great mysteries of the Nicene Creed,<|5.76|>
<|5.94|> which is one of the great summarizations of the Christian faith.<|9.26|>
<|10.16|> I want to thank Andrew or Aaron Nyquist and the people of Good Shepherd in New York for inspiring this series.<|16.90|>
<|17.72|> I'm seated because in lieu of a conventional sermon,<|21.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The clergy and I will share just a short teaching seated,<|4.18|>
<|4.76|> and perhaps if we're seated, it will be short,<|7.50|>
<|7.74|> followed by the open space for prayers,<|11.24|>
<|11.28|> at which time you're invited to wander the cathedral,<|13.76|>
<|14.82|> being conscious, especially during the season,<|16.92|>
<|16.92|> that you are guided and held by God, the Holy Trinity.<|21.94|>
<|22.72|> We begin our reflection with the first line in the creed,<|26.22|>
<|26.30|> we believe in God, the Father Almighty.<|28.36|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we sink into God, the Holy Trinity, it's important to remember that belief is never mere<|8.40|>
<|8.40|> intellectual assent. Instead, belief is a choice to trust, always a choice.<|15.72|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Rowan Williams, the Welch poet and former Archbishop of Canterbury has said that the<|5.36|>
<|5.36|> Creed is a series of statements about where I find the anchorage of my life, where I find<|12.96|>
<|12.96|> solid ground, where I find home.<|16.30|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So when we say then we believe in one God, what we're saying really is that we find our home in God, that we're anchored in God, who is our refuge, our maker, our creator, the one who holds us always.<|18.38|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And for those of us in the Anglican and Episcopal tradition, this confidence, this trust, refuge,<|7.26|>
<|7.38|> and home find their expression through us, but they have their genesis in God.<|14.34|>
<|15.06|> Before we ourselves trust God, we're going to be a part of the world.<|18.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God trusts us. Before we are ever confident in God, God is actually confident in us.<|8.72|>
<|9.30|> Before we take refuge in God, God has already taken refuge in us.<|14.92|>
<|14.92|> Before we return home to God, God is already making God's home with us and in us.<|22.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And now to the language of Father.<|3.08|>
<|4.08|> When we refer to God as the Father, the Almighty,<|6.58|>
<|7.72|> are we saying that God is literally a man above us in heaven?<|13.10|>
<|14.76|> No, all of our language for and about God is,<|18.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is an approximation, always an approximation, even when it's found in the Creed. This language gets<|8.34|>
<|8.34|> us to the edge of where God dwells. No string of words, no piece of music, no series of statements<|16.80|>
<|16.80|> adequately articulates the ineffability of the divine.<|22.26|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So instead of literalizing this imagery, the 20th century writer Marion Mix says the following,<|7.38|>
<|7.72|> for our purposes, the important thing to notice is the invitation that the title God the Father<|15.02|>
<|15.02|> offers us. And the invitation is this, come closer.<|19.06|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These two words come closer may very well be the invitation from God for all of us this evening,<|7.34|>
<|7.74|> the good God who's overflowing with goodness towards us and the whole cosmos,<|13.04|>
<|13.42|> the God who uses almighty power to fill the entire creation and even our lives with goodness.<|22.22|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thanks.<|0.50|><|endoftext|>
